items.subject: ecology
items.topic: how urban heat islands affect local ecosystems
items.transcript: Walk from a shaded city park into a downtown parking lot on a summer afternoon, and you'll feel the temperature jump several degrees in just a few steps. That gap has a name. Ecologists call it the urban heat island effect, and it describes how cities trap and re-radiate heat far more than the countryside around them. Dark asphalt and concrete absorb solar energy during the day and release it slowly at night, so a city can stay warm long after sunset while nearby farmland cools off. Now, the interesting part for an ecologist is not the discomfort, it's what this does to living communities. Consider insects. A research team tracking aphids and the ladybugs that prey on them found something unexpected in warmer city blocks. The aphids reproduced faster in the heat, but the ladybugs, which are more sensitive to temperature, became sluggish and ate less. So the predators couldn't keep up, and aphid outbreaks spread through urban gardens. The same warming also shifts the timing of biological events. Flowers in heat-island neighborhoods bloomed nearly two weeks earlier than the same species in cooler suburbs. That sounds harmless, but the pollinators that depend on those flowers don't always arrive early too. When the bloom and the bee fall out of step, both sides lose. So the heat island isn't just an engineering problem about hot pavement. It quietly rewires predator-prey balances and the seasonal clockwork that holds local ecosystems together, and it offers ecologists a kind of natural laboratory for the warming the whole planet is heading toward.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How warmer city temperatures disrupt local ecological relationships
options.B: Why asphalt and concrete absorb more heat than farmland
options.C: How cities can be redesigned to lower summer temperatures
options.D: The discomfort people feel walking through hot urban areas

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授全程在讲城市热岛效应如何改变捕食者-猎物关系和开花授粉时序，即扰乱当地生态。B只是讲热岛成因的一个细节（too_narrow），不是主旨。C讨论城市降温的工程改造，讲座并未涉及（wrong_topic）。D借用了开头'discomfort'一词，但教授明确说生态学家关心的不是不适感（surface_word_trap）。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe walking from a city park into a parking lot?

options.A: To suggest that parking lots should be built with lighter materials
options.B: To give students a familiar way to sense the temperature difference
options.C: To argue that city parks are cooler than surrounding farmland
options.D: To explain how asphalt releases stored heat after sunset

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授用这个日常场景作为开场钩子，让学生通过亲身体验直观感受到城乡温差，引出热岛概念。A把开场误读为关于建材的建议（wrong_purpose）。C扭曲了目的，且讲座比较的是城市与乡村而非公园与农田（wrong_purpose）。D是讲座后文真正说过的内容，但那不是提到散步场景的目的（literal_restatement）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the research team find about ladybugs in warmer city blocks?

options.A: They reproduced faster than they did in cooler areas
options.B: They migrated away from gardens to escape the heat
options.C: They became sluggish and ate fewer aphids
options.D: They multiplied and wiped out the aphid populations

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座明确指出瓢虫对温度更敏感，在热岛中变得迟钝、进食减少。A把蚜虫繁殖加快的事实错安到瓢虫身上（wrong_detail）。B说瓢虫迁走，讲座未提及（topic_adjacent）。D完全颠倒了因果——实际是瓢虫跟不上、蚜虫暴发，而非瓢虫消灭蚜虫（reversed_logic）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studying urban heat islands?

options.A: They are mainly a concern for city engineers rather than ecologists
options.B: They harm insects but leave plant life largely unaffected
options.C: Their effects can be reversed simply by planting more flowers
options.D: They can help scientists understand broader planetary warming

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授结尾把热岛称为应对全球变暖的'自然实验室'，暗示研究它有助于理解整个地球的升温。A被结尾明确否定——教授说这不只是工程问题（reversed_logic）。B与讲座矛盾，因为花期提前正说明植物受影响（reversed_logic）。C是相关但讲座未支持的推断，教授并未说多种花就能逆转影响（topic_adjacent）。

items.subject: neuroscience
items.topic: how mirror neurons relate to empathy and imitation
items.transcript: Have you ever winced when you watched someone else stub their toe? You weren't hurt, yet your brain reacted almost as if you were. That reaction points us toward today's topic: mirror neurons. A mirror neuron is a brain cell that fires both when you perform an action and when you simply watch someone else perform that same action. The discovery happened almost by accident. In the early 1990s, researchers in Italy were recording from the brains of macaque monkeys, studying cells that controlled hand movements. They noticed that certain neurons fired when a monkey picked up a peanut. But here's the surprise. The very same neurons also fired when the monkey just watched a researcher pick up a peanut. The monkey wasn't moving at all. Its brain was, in a sense, mirroring the action it saw. Scientists think this mirroring helps explain two very human abilities. The first is imitation. Watching someone tie a knot activates the same circuits you'd use to tie it yourself, which makes copying the motion easier to learn. The second is empathy. When you see a friend smile or flinch, mirror systems may let you internally simulate that feeling, so you understand it from the inside rather than just observing it. Of course, mirror neurons aren't the whole story of empathy, and scientists still debate exactly how much they explain. But the core idea is striking. Part of how we connect with other people may come from brain cells that blur the line between doing and watching.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How monkeys learn to pick up food in laboratory studies
options.B: Brain cells that fire when we act and when we watch
options.C: Why some people feel more empathy than others do
options.D: How researchers record electrical signals inside the brain

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。整堂课围绕镜像神经元——既在做动作时放电、也在看别人做同一动作时放电——以及它与模仿和共情的关系。A只是发现镜像神经元的实验细节（too_narrow）。C讨论人与人共情程度的差异，讲座并未比较（wrong_topic）。D借用了'record'这个词，但讲座主题不是记录脑信号的方法（surface_word_trap）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what surprised the researchers in Italy?

options.A: The monkeys could pick up peanuts faster than expected
options.B: The neurons fired only when the monkey was completely still
options.C: The same neurons fired when the monkey merely watched
options.D: The neurons stopped firing once the monkey learned the task

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座说意外之处在于：当猴子只是看研究者拿花生、自己并未动作时，同一批神经元也放电。A把动作速度当成惊喜点，讲座未提（wrong_detail）。B说神经元只在猴子静止时放电，与'既做又看都放电'矛盾（reversed_logic）。D说学会任务后神经元停止放电，是相关领域可能的想象但讲座未提（topic_adjacent）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how might mirror neurons help with imitation?

options.A: They erase the memory of actions a person has already mastered
options.B: They allow people to perform actions without ever watching them
options.C: They make watching others feel physically painful to observers
options.D: They activate the same circuits used to perform the watched action

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座说看别人打结会激活你自己打结时所用的同一回路，从而更容易学会模仿动作。A说抹去已掌握动作的记忆，讲座未提（topic_adjacent）。B说不需观看就能完成动作，与'通过观看来模仿'相反（reversed_logic）。C把开头'wince'的疼痛感错当成模仿机制（wrong_detail）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about mirror neurons and empathy?

options.A: They likely contribute to empathy but do not fully explain it
options.B: They are the single complete cause of all human empathy
options.C: They prevent people from understanding others' emotions
options.D: They are found only in monkeys and not in human brains

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授明确说镜像神经元不是共情的全部故事，科学家仍在争论它解释多少，暗示它有贡献但不充分。B把这一点夸大为'唯一完整原因'（over_generalization）。C说它阻碍理解他人情绪，与讲座所说'让你从内部模拟感受'相反（reversed_logic）。D说人类没有镜像神经元，是相关但未被支持的推断，讲座正是用它解释人类能力（topic_adjacent）。

items.subject: biology
items.transcript: Imagine an animal you can boil, freeze near absolute zero, blast with radiation, or even launch into the vacuum of space, and it just shrugs it off. That animal exists, and it's smaller than a grain of sand. We call it the tardigrade, or sometimes the water bear. Tardigrades are tiny eight-legged creatures that live in moss, soil, and water all over the planet. What makes them famous is their ability to survive conditions that would kill almost anything else. The secret isn't toughness in the ordinary sense. It's a strategy called cryptobiosis. When their environment dries out, tardigrades pull in their legs, expel most of the water from their bodies, and curl into a shriveled ball known as a tun. In this state, their metabolism nearly stops. They aren't quite dead, but they aren't really living either, just suspended. Now, how do they avoid falling apart without water? Researchers discovered they produce special proteins that, as the body dries, turn the cell's interior into a kind of glass. This glassy state locks delicate structures in place so they don't collapse or break. When water returns, sometimes years later, the glass dissolves, the tardigrade rehydrates, and it walks away as if nothing happened. This matters beyond curiosity. Scientists are studying those proteins to find better ways to preserve vaccines and other fragile biological materials without refrigeration. So a creature you can barely see may end up teaching us how to keep medicines stable in the harshest places on Earth.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Where tardigrades can be found across different habitats
options.B: How scientists store vaccines without using refrigeration
options.C: How tardigrades survive extreme conditions through cryptobiosis
options.D: Why tardigrades are smaller than a single grain of sand

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座核心是水熊虫如何通过隐生（cryptobiosis）在极端环境中存活。A只是讲它们的栖息地，属次要细节（too_narrow）。B是讲座结尾提到的应用，借用了'vaccines'一词，但不是主旨（surface_word_trap）。D只关注体型这一细节，不是主题（too_narrow）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to a tardigrade when its environment dries out?

options.A: It speeds up its metabolism to find water quickly
options.B: It grows a hard outer shell to seal in moisture
options.C: It absorbs extra water and swells to survive
options.D: It expels most of its water and curls into a tun

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座说环境干燥时，水熊虫排出体内大部分水分、蜷成称为'tun'的干球。A说加快代谢，与'代谢几乎停止'相反（reversed_logic）。B说长出硬壳锁水，讲座未提（topic_adjacent）。C说吸入额外水分膨胀，与排水缩成球完全相反（reversed_logic）。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention boiling, freezing, and radiation at the start of the lecture?

options.A: To highlight the extreme conditions the tardigrade can survive
options.B: To list the experiments that first discovered cryptobiosis
options.C: To warn that these conditions will eventually kill tardigrades
options.D: To suggest tardigrades originally came from outer space

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。开场列举煮、冻、辐射等极端折磨，是为了突出水熊虫惊人的存活能力，引出主题。B把它说成发现隐生的实验清单，讲座并无此意（literal_restatement）。C说这些条件最终会杀死水熊虫，与开场'它毫不在意'相反（wrong_purpose）。D说水熊虫来自太空，是讲座未表达的过度推断（over_inference）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the proteins tardigrades produce?

options.A: They make tardigrades grow larger when water returns
options.B: They could have practical uses in human medicine
options.C: They work only inside the tardigrade and nowhere else
options.D: They prove tardigrades are the oldest animals on Earth

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说科学家正研究这些蛋白以求在无需冷藏下保存疫苗等脆弱材料，暗示其可用于人类医学。A说蛋白让水熊虫变大，讲座未提且与复水后'若无其事走开'不符（topic_adjacent）。C说蛋白只在水熊虫体内起作用，与科学家想借用它的方向相反（reversed_logic）。D把蛋白的作用夸张成证明水熊虫是地球最古老动物（over_generalization）。

items.subject: psychology
items.transcript: Suppose you're convinced a new coworker is unfriendly. From that moment on, every time they skip a greeting, you think, see, I knew it. But the days they smile and say good morning? Those somehow slip right past you. That lopsided way of noticing is today's subject: confirmation bias. Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for, favor, and remember information that supports what we already believe, while downplaying evidence that contradicts it. It isn't lying to ourselves on purpose. It's a quiet filter running in the background of ordinary thinking. A classic study makes this vivid. Researchers gave people a number sequence, two, four, six, and asked them to guess the rule behind it. Most decided the rule was even numbers counting up, and then they only tested examples that fit that guess, like eight, ten, twelve. They kept getting yes for an answer, so they felt confident. But the actual rule was simply any increasing numbers. Because they only tested cases meant to confirm their idea, they never tried something like one, two, three, which would have revealed the truth. Confirmation bias matters far beyond puzzles. A doctor who settles on a diagnosis early may notice symptoms that fit it and overlook ones that don't. An investor convinced a stock will rise may read only the rosy forecasts. The danger isn't that we have beliefs. It's that we rarely go looking for the evidence that could prove us wrong. And recognizing that tendency is the first step toward thinking more carefully.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why first impressions of coworkers are usually accurate
options.B: How doctors and investors are trained to make decisions
options.C: A famous number puzzle used in psychology classes
options.D: A mental tendency to favor evidence that fits our beliefs

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。整堂课讲确认偏误——倾向于寻找并记住支持已有信念的信息。A误读了开场的同事例子，讲座并未说第一印象准确（surface_word_trap）。B借用了医生和投资者的例子，但讲座讲的是偏误而非他们如何受训（surface_word_trap）。C只关注数字谜题这个例子，不是主旨（too_narrow）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was the actual rule in the number sequence study?

options.A: Any sequence of numbers that keeps increasing
options.B: Even numbers counting upward by twos
options.C: Numbers that all share a common factor
options.D: Numbers that repeat the pattern two, four, six

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。讲座说真正的规则只是'任何递增的数字'。B是参与者错误猜测的规则，而非真实规则（wrong_detail）。C说共同因数，讲座从未提及（topic_adjacent）。D说重复二四六的模式，是把示例当规则的误读（wrong_detail）。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the doctor and the investor?

options.A: To claim that experts are immune to confirmation bias
options.B: To show that confirmation bias matters in real, high-stakes situations
options.C: To explain how doctors and investors are professionally trained
options.D: To argue that puzzles are more reliable than real-world judgments

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授举医生和投资者，是为了说明确认偏误不止存在于谜题里，还会影响现实中关系重大的决策。A说专家不受偏误影响，与例子相反（wrong_purpose）。C把例子误读为讲职业培训（literal_restatement）。D说谜题比现实判断更可靠，是讲座未表达的过度推断（over_inference）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply is the key to reducing confirmation bias?

options.A: Avoiding having any strong beliefs in the first place
options.B: Trusting one's initial impressions more confidently
options.C: Deliberately seeking evidence that could prove us wrong
options.D: Relying on experts to make important decisions for us

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说危险在于我们很少去找能推翻自己的证据，并称认识到这一点是更谨慎思考的第一步，暗示应主动寻找反证。A说干脆不要有信念，但教授明说问题不在于有信念（reversed_logic）。B说更自信地相信初始印象，正会加重偏误（reversed_logic）。D说靠专家代为决策，是讲座未支持的相关推断（topic_adjacent）。
